data.title: FDA and OpenAI meeting to discuss AI in drug evaluation - Wired
data.description:
The U.S. Food and Drug Administration is holding talks with OpenAI, the Microsoft (NASDAQ:MSFT) backed generative artificial intelligence startup, to discuss the agency’s use of AI, according to Wired citing sources.

The sources, who are close to the project, say a small team from OpenAI has met with the FDA and two associates of Elon Musk's Department of Government Efficiency multiple times in recent weeks.

The report also mentioned that the group has discussed a project called cderGPT, or Center for Drug Evaluation, which regulates over-the-counter and prescription drugs in the U.S.

Jeremy Walsh, who was recently named as the FDA’s first-ever AI officer, lead the discussions. 

Possibly hinting at this agreement, FDA commissioner Marty Makary posted on X on Wednesday, “Why does it take over 10 years for a new drug to come to market? Why are we not modernized with AI and other things? We’ve just completed our first AI-assisted scientific review for a product, and that’s just the beginning.” 

Wired mentioned in its report that no contract has been signed yet.

data.title: Schrödinger projects strong 2025 revenue growth with $45M-$50M in drug discovery
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Schrödinger, Inc. (SDGR) Q1 2025

Management View

  • CEO Ramy Farid highlighted strong Q1 performance, reporting total revenue of $59.6 million, driven by $48.8 million in software revenue with 46% growth and $10.7 million in drug discovery revenue. Farid reiterated confidence in the full-year 2025 guidance and emphasized the continued adoption of their computational drug discovery platform.
  • Farid announced advancements in predictive toxicology solutions, with over 50 off-targets structurally enabled, and plans to release a beta version later this year. New capabilities were also introduced in protein degrader modeling, T-cell receptor structure prediction, and crystal structure prediction for drug formulation.
  • CFO Geoff Porges stated that operating expenses declined year-over-year, and the cash position improved significantly to $512 million, supported by collections from contracts and an upfront payment from Novartis.

Outlook

  • Management reiterated full-year 2025 guidance, projecting software revenue growth of 10%-15% and drug discovery revenue in the range of $45 million to $50 million. Software gross margins are expected to remain between 74%-75%.
  • For Q2 2025, software revenue is forecasted to range between $38 million and $42 million, with drug discovery revenue expected to be distributed evenly over the remaining quarters.

Financial Results

  • Total revenue for Q1 2025 was $59.6 million, up 63% year-over-year, with software revenue contributing $48.8 million and drug discovery revenue $10.7 million. These results included higher recognition of revenue from collaborations, particularly with Novartis.
  • Software gross margin was 72%, impacted by the Gates Foundation-funded Predictive Tox Initiative. Overall gross margin remained stable at 52%, consistent with Q1 2024.
  • Net loss for the quarter was $60 million, or $0.82 per share, reflecting higher total expenses and a loss in the fair value of equity method investments.

Q&A

  • Michael Yee, Jefferies: Asked about expectations for SGR-1505 data. Karen Akinsanya responded that the initial update will describe safety, pharmacokinetics, and preliminary efficacy data from the Phase 1 trial.
  • Brendan Smith, TD Cowen: Inquired about the predictive tox initiative. CEO Farid emphasized the differentiation of their solution, which integrates physics and machine learning for higher accuracy, and noted plans to determine pricing after beta testing.
  • Mani Foroohar, Leerink Partners: Questioned competition and AI threats. Farid explained the necessity of training sets for machine learning and assured that the company’s physics-based methods remain robust against potential disruptions.
  • Vikram Purohit, Morgan Stanley: Asked about the potential for partnering SGR-1505 post-data release. Akinsanya stated that Phase 1 data would guide further partnering discussions later this year.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts primarily focused on the clinical pipeline, financial guidance, and software offerings. The tone was generally positive, reflecting confidence in the company's progress and strategic direction.
  • Management maintained an optimistic tone, emphasizing strong financial results and advancements in technology. However, caution was expressed regarding the macroeconomic environment.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analysts and management displayed consistent confidence, with an added focus on the upcoming clinical data and predictive toxicology solutions.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Revenue increased significantly in Q1 2025 compared to Q4 2024, driven by strong software and drug discovery performance.
  • Management reiterated unchanged guidance for 2025, consistent with the previous quarter's outlook.
  • Strategic focus shifted slightly towards highlighting predictive toxicology and upcoming clinical trial data, reflecting progress in these areas.
  • Analysts continued to focus on financial sustainability and the impact of new product offerings, similar to concerns raised in Q4 2024.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management noted potential impacts from macroeconomic challenges and political uncertainties but stated that the business remains relatively protected.
  • Analyst concerns centered around the scalability of the predictive tox initiative and the ability to sustain revenue growth in a competitive landscape.
  • Management emphasized the importance of their cash position and technology differentiation as mitigating factors.

Final Takeaway

Schrödinger demonstrated a strong start to 2025, with notable revenue growth and advancements in software and drug discovery. The company reiterated its full-year guidance and highlighted progress in predictive toxicology and clinical programs, positioning it for continued growth and innovation. Upcoming clinical data releases and the beta launch of the predictive tox solution are expected to be key catalysts for the year.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Barrick Gold outlines strategic focus on Tier 1 assets and copper growth in Q1 2025
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Barrick Gold Corporation (GOLD) Q1 2025

Management View

  • CEO Mark Bristow emphasized Barrick's long-term strategy, focusing on Tier 1 gold assets and expanding its copper portfolio. He announced the company's name change to Barrick Mining Corporation, reflecting this strategic focus. The ticker symbol on the NYSE will also be updated to "B."
  • The company reported production at the top end of guidance, maintaining a dividend of $0.10 per share and advancing its $1 billion share buyback program. Bristow highlighted the $1 billion sale of Donlin and a process to evaluate Hemlo as part of portfolio rationalization.
  • Significant project milestones include Pueblo Viejo facility upgrades, the transition of Lumwana and Reko Diq to construction phase, and advancing the Fourmile project to feasibility study. Copper production was strong, supporting the company's dual focus on gold and copper.
  • CFO Graham Shuttleworth noted the quarter's financial resilience, citing growth in operating cash flow, free cash flow, and earnings, despite temporary operational challenges.

Outlook

  • Barrick reiterated its full-year production targets for gold and copper. Pueblo Viejo is expected to ramp up production beyond 800,000 ounces per year by 2026, supported by ongoing expansion projects.
  • The company projected substantial long-term potential for the Reko Diq project in Pakistan, with a 37-year reserve life and anticipated capital investment for Phase 1 of $1.4-$1.7 billion. Phase 2 is expected to be self-funded through project cash flow.
  • The strategic divestment of non-core assets, including Donlin and potentially Hemlo, was emphasized as aligning with the focus on Tier 1 operations.

Financial Results

  • The quarter saw significant improvements in financial performance, with all key metrics showing growth. Specific financial figures such as operating cash flow and earnings were attributed to higher gold prices and operational efficiency.
  • Capital expenses were reported to be on track with projections, with growth capital set to increase as major projects progress.
  • The company highlighted the realized gold price for the quarter, which was considered conservative in light of current market conditions.

Q&A

  • Ralph Profiti (Stifel) questioned the valuation correlation between Donlin's sale and Fourmile's integration into Nevada Gold Mines. CEO Bristow clarified that Donlin was not competitive for capital allocation, emphasizing Fourmile's superior grade and operational advantages.
  • Brian MacArthur (Raymond James) inquired about the economic benefits of sulfur tailings processing in Nevada. Henri Gonin detailed cost savings, highlighting a production cost of $70 per ton versus $300 per ton for purchased sulfur.
  • Josh Wolfson (RBC) asked about potential copper M&A opportunities. Bristow noted the strategic advantage of investing in copper projects viable at lower prices, citing Lumwana and Reko Diq as examples.
  • Tanya Jakusconek (Scotiabank) sought clarity on succession planning and the status of Hemlo and other non-core assets. Management reiterated its structured approach to leadership transitions and strategic divestments.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts displayed interest in the company's strategic shifts, particularly the focus on Tier 1 assets and copper projects. Concerns were raised regarding asset divestments and operational risks in Mali.
  • Management maintained a confident tone, emphasizing disciplined capital allocation and long-term growth potential. CEO Bristow consistently highlighted the company’s strategic clarity and operational resilience.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, the tone was slightly more proactive, reflecting confidence in ongoing projects and strategic decisions.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter saw a continuation of strategic initiatives from Q4 2024, with a stronger emphasis on divesting non-core assets to optimize the portfolio.
  • Guidance remained consistent, with reaffirmed production targets and clarity on project milestones for Pueblo Viejo, Reko Diq, and Lumwana.
  • Analysts remained focused on capital allocation and operational efficiencies, similar to the previous quarter.

Risks and Concerns

  • Political risks in Mali were addressed, with operations at Loulo-Gounkoto under suspension. Management is actively negotiating with authorities to resolve disputes and ensure long-term sustainability.
  • Operational challenges at Pueblo Viejo and Nevada Gold Mines were highlighted, with planned maintenance impacting production temporarily.
  • The company noted potential delays in project execution due to external factors but emphasized mitigation strategies.

Final Takeaway

Barrick Gold Corporation is advancing its strategy of focusing on Tier 1 gold assets and expanding its copper portfolio. The sale of non-core assets like Donlin and the initiation of major projects such as Reko Diq and Pueblo Viejo's expansion underline its commitment to long-term value creation. Despite operational challenges and geopolitical risks, the company maintains a confident outlook, supported by disciplined capital allocation and robust project pipelines.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Gold slides on U.S.-China trade talk hopes but analysts say still well-supported
data.description:
Gold futures pulled back Wednesday, weighed by a stronger dollar and easing China-U.S. trade tensions, after rallying to a record high in the previous session.

The U.S. dollar and stocks gained on news that a team led by Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent and U.S. Trade Representative Jamieson Greer will meet their Chinese counterparts this weekend for initial talks, improving risk appetite in the market.

Analysts said traders took the apparent softening in hardline attitudes as a reason to offload some safe-haven positions and increase risk exposure, and a downward correction or at least a period of consolidation is reasonable given the size of Monday and Tuesday's gains.

The resumption of trade talks may not lead to any substantive outcome, and gold's safe-haven status remains well supported due to lingering geopolitical instability, an uncertain trade environment and expectations for U.S. rate cuts later this year, XS.com's Linh Tran said.

Gold pared some of its earlier losses after the Federal Reserve kept interest rates unchanged, warning of uncertainty to the economy stemming from tariffs, and Chair Jerome Powell said the central bank cannot act pre-emptively when the path forward is unclear.

Front-month Comex gold (XAUUSD:CUR) for May delivery ended -0.9% to $3,381.40/oz, still the fourth highest close in history, and front-month Comex silver (XAGUSD:CUR) for May delivery finished -1.7% to $32.531/oz.

ETFs: (NYSEARCA:GLD), (NYSEARCA:GDX), (GDXJ), (IAU), (NUGT), (PHYS), (GLDM), (AAAU), (SGOL), (RING), (BAR), (OUNZ), (SLV), (PSLV), (SIVR), (SIL), (SILJ)

data.title: Manulife Financial Q1 profit beats even as credit loss, California wildfire provisions dampen growth
data.description:
Manulife Financial's (NYSE:MFC) Q1 2025 profit, released Wednesday, came in better than expected even as provisions for credit losses and the California wildfires dampened core earnings growth.

"Our underlying business growth remained resilient, while our core EPS growth was dampened by strengthened provisions related to expected credit loss and a provision for the California wildfires," said CFO Colin Simpson.

Still, the Canadian-based company kicked off "the year with continued strong momentum, delivering record levels of insurance new business results this quarter," said CEO Roy Gori.

Q1 2025 core EPS C$0.99 (US$0.72), slightly exceeding the C$0.98 consensus, edged down from C$1.03 in the prior three-month period, but rose from C$0.91 in Q1 2024.

MFC shares inched up 0.6% in after-hours trading.

Global WAM net inflows totaled C$0.5B in Q1, down from C$1.2B in Q4 and C$6.7B in Q1 2024.

Manulife (NYSE:MFC) strengthened its provisions associated with expected credit loss of $45M post-tax in Q1, compared with a net release of $8M post-tax in Q1 2024. It also recorded a provision for the California wildfires of $43M post-tax in Q1.

New business CSM advanced to C$907M in Q1 from C$842M in Q4 and C$658M in last year's Q1.

New business value was C$907M in Q1, up from C$842M in the earlier quarter and C$641M a year before.
